ANAT-1.w: hyper- = above/excessive normal (e.g. hypertension = high BP). Opposite of hypo- (below normal).

ANAT-3.w: -aemia/-emia = a condition of the blood (e.g. anaemia, hyperglycaemia).

ANAT-5.w: posterior = towards the back (dorsal). Anterior = front.

ANAT-6.w: lateral = away from the midline; medial = towards it.

ANAT-7.w: medial means nearer the midline, so the sternum sits closer to the body's midline than the ribs.

ANAT-8.w: proximal = nearer the trunk/point of attachment; distal = further away.

ANAT-9.w: superficial = near the surface; deep = further in.

ANAT-10.w: Good documentation uses precise directional terms (anterior aspect, left lower leg), not vague words.

ANAT-11.w: The cranial cavity (part of the dorsal cavity) houses the brain.

ANAT-12.w: The diaphragm separates the thoracic cavity (above) from the abdominal cavity (below).

ANAT-13.w: The thoracic cavity contains the heart and lungs (stomach/intestines are abdominal).

ANAT-14.w: The mediastinum is the central compartment between the lungs; it contains the heart, great vessels, trachea and oesophagus.

ANAT-15.w: The pelvic cavity holds the bladder, rectum and reproductive organs.

ANAT-16.w: The thoracic cavity protects the lungs (breathing) and heart (circulation).

ANAT-17.w: Levels of organisation: chemical → cells → tissues → organs → organ systems → organism.

ANAT-18.w: An organ system is a group of organs working together (e.g. respiratory system). A lung alone is an organ.

CELLFN-1.w: Dendrites receive incoming signals; the single axon sends signals out. More dendrites = more inputs collected.

CELLFN-2.w: The cytoskeleton — mainly microtubules — gives a cell its shape and internal structure.

CELLFN-3.w: Mitochondria are the 'powerhouse': they produce ATP via aerobic respiration.

CELLFN-4.r: Matching: Red blood cell → biconcave shape; Phagocyte → many lysosomes (to digest microbes); Neuron → myelinated axon; Melanocyte → melanosomes (make melanin).
CELLFN-4.q: Give the key structural feature of each cell type: red blood cell, phagocyte, neuron, melanocyte.

CELLFN-5.w: Lysosomes contain digestive enzymes and acid; phagocytes have many to break down engulfed microbes.

CELLFN-6.w: Membrane proteins do all of these: adhesion, signalling, facilitated diffusion, and cell recognition.

CELLFN-7.w: Cytoplasm = everything inside the cell membrane except the nucleus: the cytosol PLUS all organelles and inclusions. (Cytosol alone is just the fluid.)

CELLDIV-1.q: Skin repairs itself by mitosis. What are the two steps of mitosis, and what is the phase called when a cell is NOT dividing?
CELLDIV-1.r: Mitosis = karyokinesis (the nucleus/genetic material divides) then cytokinesis (the cytoplasm splits) — giving two identical daughter cells. A non-dividing cell is in INTERPHASE, when it grows and copies its DNA ready for the next division.
CELLDIV-1.w: Interphase is the longest part of the cell cycle.

CELLDIV-2.r: Stages of mitosis: PROPHASE — chromatin coils into visible chromosomes (paired chromatids); centrioles form spindle fibres. METAPHASE — chromosomes line up on the middle (equator) of the spindle. ANAPHASE — centromeres split; chromatids pulled to opposite poles. TELOPHASE — new nuclear membranes form around each set; cytoplasm divides (cytokinesis) into two identical cells.

CELLDIV-3.q: In mitosis, name the stage where chromatids are (a) lined up on the spindle equator, and (b) being pulled to opposite poles. Then list the four stages in order.
CELLDIV-3.r: (a) On the equator = METAPHASE. (b) Moving to the poles = ANAPHASE. Order: Prophase, Metaphase, Anaphase, Telophase.
CELLDIV-3.w: Mnemonic PMAT: 'Met in the Middle, pulled Apart in Anaphase.'

CELLDIV-4.q: Give the single identifying feature that tells each mitosis stage apart: prophase, metaphase, anaphase, telophase.
CELLDIV-4.r: Prophase = chromosomes condense and become visible, no equator line yet. Metaphase = chromosomes lined up single-file on the equator. Anaphase = sister chromatids split and move to opposite poles. Telophase = two new nuclei reform and the cytoplasm divides (cytokinesis).
CELLDIV-4.w: Look for the equator line: on it = metaphase, split from it = anaphase.

CELLDIV-5.q: Contrast mitosis and meiosis on four points: where it happens, number of daughter cells, chromosome number of the daughters, and its purpose.
CELLDIV-5.r: MITOSIS: happens in all body tissues; 2 daughter cells; full chromosome number (diploid, 46 in humans); for growth and repair. MEIOSIS: only in ovaries/testes; 4 daughter cells; HALF the chromosomes (haploid, 23); makes gametes for sexual reproduction ('reduction division').
CELLDIV-5.w: Halving in meiosis means fertilisation restores 46 (23 + 23).

MEMB-1.w: Osmosis = movement of WATER across a selectively permeable membrane, from low to high solute concentration.

MEMB-2.w: Concentration = number of solute particles per unit volume of solvent.

MEMB-3.w: Osmolarity = the total concentration of solute particles in a solution.

MEMB-4.w: Simple diffusion = small non-polar molecules (like O2) crossing the lipid bilayer directly, no protein, no ATP.

MEMB-5.w: Diffusion is driven by the concentration gradient (high → low). No ATP needed.

MEMB-6.w: Facilitated diffusion uses membrane transport proteins; it is still passive (down the gradient, no ATP).

MEMB-7.w: Large or charged/polar molecules can't cross the lipid core, so they need protein-assisted (facilitated) transport.

MEMB-8.w: Ion channels allow passive movement of ions down their gradient (no ATP).

MEMB-9.w: GLUT4 is the insulin-responsive glucose transporter in muscle and fat cells.

MEMB-10.w: When a solution is over-saturated, solutes can precipitate out — e.g. kidney stones.

MEMB-11.w: Hypotonic outside = fewer solutes outside, so water moves IN and the cell swells (may burst).

MEMB-12.w: Hypertonic = higher solute concentration than inside the cell, so water leaves and the cell shrinks.

MEMB-13.w: Diffusion moves solutes; osmosis specifically moves water across a selectively permeable membrane.

MEMB-14.w: Both are passive/down-gradient, but simple diffusion needs no protein whereas facilitated diffusion requires specific membrane proteins.

MEMB-15.w: Insulin triggers signalling that moves GLUT4 vesicles to insert into the membrane, increasing glucose uptake.

MEMB-16.w: Active transport pumps substances AGAINST their gradient, so it must spend ATP.

MEMB-17.w: The Na+/K+ pump moves 3 Na+ OUT and 2 K+ IN per ATP used.

MEMB-18.w: Ion channels facilitate the passive movement of ions down their gradient.

TIS1-1.q: Name the layers of the epidermis from deepest to most superficial. Which layer divides, and which is dead? Then: what tissue makes up the dermis and the hypodermis?
TIS1-1.r: Epidermis deep to superficial: stratum BASALE (dividing stem cells) - SPINOSUM - GRANULOSUM - LUCIDUM (thick skin only) - CORNEUM (dead, keratin-filled, waterproof surface). The dermis = dense IRREGULAR connective tissue. The hypodermis = mostly ADIPOSE (fat) tissue.
TIS1-1.w: Only the basale divides; cells flatten, fill with keratin and die as they rise.

TIS1-2.q: Why is the epidermis classed as 'stratified squamous' epithelium, and where in it do you find the melanocytes and the dividing cells?
TIS1-2.r: Stratified = many cell layers; squamous = the surface cells are flat — so 'stratified squamous'. The melanocytes (make melanin for UV protection) and the dividing stem cells both sit in the deepest layer, the stratum basale.
TIS1-2.w: As cells are pushed up they flatten, keratinise and die.

TIS1-3.q: Dense irregular connective tissue: where is it in the skin, how are its collagen fibres arranged, and why does that suit the dermis?
TIS1-3.r: Found in the DERMIS (deep to the epidermis). Its collagen fibres run in many directions (criss-crossed / irregular), so it resists pulling forces from ALL directions — ideal for skin that gets stretched every way.
TIS1-3.w: Contrast dense REGULAR (parallel fibres) in tendons — strong in one direction only.

TIS1-4.q: Loose (areolar) connective tissue: how do you recognise it, where is it found, and what does it do?
TIS1-4.r: Looks loose and open — sparse cells and scattered collagen + elastic fibres in a gel matrix, with fibroblasts. Found under epithelia, around vessels and organs, and in the hypodermis. It holds structures in place, cushions them, and is the main site of inflammation and immune defence.

TIS1-5.q: Adipose tissue: how do you recognise it under the microscope, where is the fat stored in each cell, and what are its roles besides storing energy?
TIS1-5.r: Cells look like empty rings ('signet-ring'): one big fat droplet fills the cell and pushes the nucleus and cytoplasm to the rim. Besides energy storage it provides insulation (heat), cushioning/protection of organs, and acts as an endocrine tissue (e.g. releases leptin).
TIS1-5.w: The 'empty' look is because the fat washes out during slide prep.

TIS1-6.q: Which muscle type is the arrector pili (the skin muscle that raises hairs / causes goosebumps), and what does it do?
TIS1-6.r: SMOOTH muscle (involuntary). It contracts to pull the hair upright and dimple the skin ('goosebumps'), and helps squeeze sebum out of the sebaceous glands.
TIS1-6.w: Smooth muscle: spindle-shaped fibres, one central nucleus, no stripes/striations.

TIS1-7.q: Blood is a connective tissue. Name the granulocytes and the agranulocytes with one function each, and say why blood counts as a connective tissue.
TIS1-7.r: Granulocytes: NEUTROPHILS (first responders, engulf bacteria), EOSINOPHILS (parasites and allergy), BASOPHILS (release histamine for inflammation). Agranulocytes: LYMPHOCYTES (B cells make antibodies, T cells kill infected cells), MONOCYTES (become macrophages). Blood is a connective tissue because it is cells suspended in a large extracellular MATRIX (plasma).
TIS1-7.w: 'Never Let Monkeys Eat Bananas' — neutrophils, lymphocytes, monocytes, eosinophils, basophils by frequency.

TIS1-8.q: Name the parts of a neuron and their jobs, say what the neuroglia do, and state what is damaged in multiple sclerosis (MS).
TIS1-8.r: CELL BODY (holds the nucleus, integrates signals) - DENDRITES (receive incoming signals) - AXON (carries the impulse away), usually wrapped in a MYELIN sheath that speeds conduction. Neuroglia are support cells (astrocytes support/feed, oligodendrocytes make myelin, microglia = immune defence, ependymal make CSF). In MS the MYELIN is destroyed, so impulse conduction is impaired.
TIS1-8.w: Grey matter = cell bodies; white matter = myelinated axons.

TIS2-1.w: The respiratory tract is lined by pseudostratified ciliated columnar epithelium (traps and sweeps mucus).

TIS2-2.w: Simple squamous epithelium is thin and flat — ideal for diffusion and filtration (e.g. alveoli, capillaries).

TIS2-3.w: Connective tissue characteristically has abundant extracellular matrix between its cells.

TIS2-4.w: Adipose tissue stores energy as fat.

TIS2-5.w: Skeletal muscle is voluntary; cardiac and smooth are involuntary.

TIS2-6.w: Neurons transmit electrical signals; neuroglia support them.

TIS2-7.w: Neurons are specialised to conduct electrical impulses. (Most mature neurons can't divide.)

TIS2-8.w: The epidermis (outer skin) is stratified squamous epithelium.

TIS2-9.w: Simple columnar epithelium lines the intestines for secretion and absorption.

TIS2-10.w: Intercalated discs are unique to cardiac muscle (electrically couple the cells).

TIS2-11.w: Bone has a hard matrix due to calcium salts (hydroxyapatite).

TIS2-12.w: Regeneration = replacement of lost/damaged tissue with the same tissue type.

TIS2-13.w: The basement membrane anchors epithelium to the underlying connective tissue.

TIS2-14.w: Fibroblasts produce the extracellular matrix and collagen fibres of connective tissue.

TIS2-15.w: Fibrosis = formation of scar (fibrous) tissue after injury.

TIS2-16.q: Under the microscope, give the one identifying feature that distinguishes each: adipose tissue, blood, cartilage, and lung tissue.
TIS2-16.r: Adipose = empty 'signet-ring' cells (fat droplet, nucleus pushed to the rim). Blood = many red cells with scattered white cells in fluid. Cartilage = chondrocytes sitting in lacunae (little spaces) within a firm matrix. Lung = lots of open airspaces (alveoli).
TIS2-16.w: Ask: cells-in-fluid (blood), cells-in-lacunae (cartilage), fat-rings (adipose), or airspaces (lung)?

TIS2-17.q: How do you tell reticular tissue, hyaline cartilage and elastic cartilage apart under the microscope?
TIS2-17.r: Reticular tissue (spleen, lymph nodes) = a fine mesh of fibres packed with lymphocytes. Hyaline cartilage = smooth, glassy matrix with chondrocytes in lacunae. Elastic cartilage = the same but with obvious wavy elastic fibres in the matrix (e.g. the ear).
TIS2-17.w: Elastic vs hyaline: look for the wavy elastic fibres.

TIS2-18.q: Connective-tissue fibre arrangement: how do dense regular, dense irregular and loose connective tissue differ, and give an example of each?
TIS2-18.r: Dense REGULAR = fibres in tight parallel bundles - tendons/ligaments (strong in one direction). Dense IRREGULAR = fibres criss-crossed in all directions - dermis (strong in every direction). LOOSE (areolar) = few, widely spaced fibres with open gaps - sits under epithelia.
TIS2-18.w: Regular = one-way pull; irregular = multi-way pull; loose = packing/cushion.

TIS2-19.q: Name the four main epithelial types by their shape/layers, and where each is typically found.
TIS2-19.r: Simple squamous = single flat layer (alveoli, capillaries — thin for diffusion). Simple cuboidal = single cube-shaped layer (kidney tubules, glands). Simple columnar = single tall layer (gut lining — absorption). Stratified squamous = many layers, flat on top (skin, mouth — protection).

TIS2-20.q: How can a tissue slide tell you which organ it came from? Give two worked examples.
TIS2-20.r: Match the tissue to where that tissue lives. E.g. simple columnar epithelium with finger-like VILLI = small INTESTINE; many thin-walled airspaces (alveoli) = LUNG; chondrocytes in lacunae in a smooth matrix = cartilage (e.g. trachea or a joint).
TIS2-20.w: Identify the tissue first, then recall which organ uses it.

MIC1-1.w: True — bacteria are prokaryotes (no membrane-bound nucleus).

MIC1-2.w: True — 'prokaryote' means 'before nucleus'; their DNA is free in the cytoplasm.

MIC1-3.w: A pathogen is a disease-causing organism.

MIC1-4.w: Bacteria reproduce asexually by binary fission (one cell splits into two).

MIC1-5.w: Bacteria need the right pH, temperature, oxygen level and moisture. (The '37°C, neutral, water+oxygen' option is only true for some, not all bacteria.)

MIC1-6.w: Viral replication order: attach (3) → enter (6) → uncoat, nucleic acid enters (4) → copy nucleic acid + make proteins (1) → assemble (2) → release (5).

MIC1-7.q: Under the microscope, how do you tell bacteria, fungi and protozoa apart?
MIC1-7.r: BACTERIA = tiny single cells, simple shapes (cocci/round, bacilli/rods, spirals), with NO nucleus (prokaryotes). FUNGI = larger; either threads (hyphae/mould) or budding yeast cells, WITH a nucleus. PROTOZOA = larger single-celled 'animal-like' eukaryotes, often seen moving (e.g. amoeba), WITH a nucleus.
MIC1-7.w: Size and a nucleus are the giveaways: bacteria are smallest and have no nucleus.

MIC1-8.q: Compare bacteria, protozoa, fungi and viruses by size and structure — which is smallest, and which are actually cells?
MIC1-8.r: Size, smallest to largest: VIRUS < BACTERIA < FUNGI / PROTOZOA. Viruses are NOT cells (just genetic material in a protein coat, needing a host). Bacteria are cells but prokaryotic (no nucleus). Fungi and protozoa are eukaryotic cells (have a nucleus).
MIC1-8.w: Only viruses are non-cellular and can't reproduce on their own.

MIC1-9.q: Is a virus a living cell? Describe its basic structure and why it needs a host.
MIC1-9.r: A virus is NOT a cell and is not considered fully alive on its own. It is just nucleic acid (DNA or RNA) inside a protein coat (capsid), sometimes with an outer envelope. It cannot make energy or reproduce itself, so it must hijack a host cell's machinery to replicate.
MIC1-9.w: This is why antibiotics (which target bacterial cells) don't work on viruses.

MIC2-1.w: Obligate aerobe = can ONLY grow with oxygen.

MIC2-2.w: An amoeba moves by extending pseudopodia ('false feet') of cytoplasm.

MIC2-3.w: Obligate anaerobes (like tetanus/Clostridium) grow ONLY where there is no oxygen.

MIC2-4.w: An antibiotic is made by one microbe to kill/inhibit another (e.g. Penicillium → penicillin).

MIC2-5.w: An antigen is a foreign substance that triggers an immune response.

MIC2-6.w: An antiseptic kills/removes microbes on skin or living tissue (a disinfectant is for surfaces).

MIC2-7.w: The scabies mite is an arthropod (a vector that can carry infection).

MIC2-8.w: Aseptic technique = procedures that minimise the transfer of microorganisms.

MIC2-9.w: Asexual reproduction = one parent → genetically identical offspring (clones).

MIC2-10.w: Tinea (athlete's foot / ringworm) is a fungal infection.

MIC2-11.w: An autoclave sterilises using pressurised steam (121°C).

MIC2-12.w: Lymphocytes make antibodies (B-cells) and kill infected cells (T-cells).

MIC2-14.w: Gram-positive stains purple; bacilli are rod-shaped ('long, skinny').

MIC2-15.w: Streptococci are bacteria = prokaryotes. (Scabies, tinea, influenza are not bacteria.)

MIC2-16.w: A bacteriophage is a virus that infects bacteria.

MIC2-17.w: Binary fission = a bacterium splits into two to reproduce.

MIC2-18.w: A boil is a deep, pus-filled hair-follicle infection (usually Staphylococcus).

MIC2-19.w: Broad-spectrum antibiotics kill a wide range of bacteria — including helpful normal flora.

MIC2-20.w: Candida is a yeast (fungus), so it needs antifungal medication.

MIC2-21.w: A carrier harbours a pathogen without symptoms but can still infect others.

MIC2-22.w: Cell walls occur in fungal, plant and bacterial cells (animal cells have none).

MIC2-23.w: Chicken pox (varicella) is a viral infection.

MIC2-24.w: Hepatitis is viral. (Tonsillitis often bacterial, ringworm fungal, chlamydia bacterial.)

MIC2-25.w: Contagious = EASILY transmitted host-to-host (a subset of communicable).

MIC2-26.w: Conjugation = bacteria transfer DNA via a pilus.

MIC2-27.w: Skin-to-skin (arm brushing arm) = direct contact transmission.

MIC2-28.w: Maggot (larval) debridement removes dead/necrotic tissue from a wound.

MIC2-29.w: Defensins are antimicrobial chemicals made by skin (and other epithelia).

MIC2-31.w: Disease = a harmful alteration to normal physiology/metabolism.

MIC2-32.w: Disinfection = chemical removal/destruction of microbes from non-living surfaces.

MIC2-33.w: Airborne droplets usually come from sneezing (or coughing).

MIC2-34.w: An exogenous infection comes from outside the body (soil into the eye).

MIC2-35.w: Some Gram-positive bacteria form endospores to survive hostile conditions.

MIC2-36.w: A pyrogen is a fever-inducing substance.

MIC2-37.r: Select all: FUNGI and MOULD. Hyphae are the thread-like filaments that make up fungi/moulds.

MIC2-38.w: Fimbriae are short hair-like structures that help bacteria ADHERE to surfaces. (Pili are for conjugation.)

MIC2-39.w: Flukes and tapeworms are helminths (parasitic worms).

MIC2-40.w: A fomite is a non-living object that carries microbes (e.g. a dirty mop).

MIC2-41.w: Penicillium is a fungus: eukaryotic, has a cell wall, and cannot photosynthesise.

MIC2-42.w: A slime layer/capsule of polysaccharide helps bacteria resist phagocytosis.

MIC2-45.w: Incidence rate = the rate of NEW cases in a population over a set time.

MIC2-46.w: Incubation period = time between exposure and the appearance of symptoms.

MIC2-47.w: Antibodies present but no symptoms suggests a latent (dormant/sub-clinical) infection.

MIC2-48.w: Malaria is caused by a protozoan (Plasmodium), spread by mosquitoes.

MIC2-49.w: The lower respiratory tract is normally sterile. (Skin, mouth and genitalia carry normal flora.)

MIC2-51.w: Morbidity = the incidence/level of disease in a population.

MIC2-52.w: Non-communicable diseases are not spread person-to-person.

MIC2-53.w: Normal flora = microbes that normally live in/on the body without causing disease.

MIC2-54.w: An opportunistic infection strikes when the immune system is weakened (e.g. Candida).

MIC2-55.w: A pandemic is an epidemic that has spread worldwide.

MIC2-56.w: An ectoparasite lives ON the host's outside, feeding without killing it (e.g. lice, fleas).

MIC2-57.w: A portal of entry is where a pathogen gets in — e.g. an uncovered surgical wound.

MIC2-58.r: Select all: (b) antibiotics were sometimes given incorrectly for viral infections, and (c) people don't finish the full course. Both drive antibiotic resistance.

MIC2-59.w: A bacterial infection on top of the existing viral cold sore = a secondary infection.

MIC2-60.w: Signs are measurable/observable (BP, rash); symptoms are what the patient reports (pain, nausea).

MIC2-61.w: Believing meat turns into maggots = spontaneous generation (disproved theory).

MIC2-62.w: Sterilisation = complete destruction of ALL microbes, including viruses and endospores.

MIC2-63.w: Antibiotics wiping out normal flora → Candida overgrowth = a superinfection.

MIC2-64.w: Toxins are microbial substances that damage host cells/tissues.

MIC2-65.w: Transients are microbes present only briefly and don't cause disease.

MIC2-67.w: Zone of inhibition = the clear ring on an agar plate where antibiotic has killed the bacteria.

MIC2-68.w: Bacteria and fungi use extracellular digestion — they secrete enzymes then absorb the products.

MIC2-69.w: A virus: not killed by antibiotics, hides inside cells, mutates into strains, hard to target without harming the host.

MIC2-70.w: A virus is basically a protein coat (capsid) around nucleic acid — the simplest microbe.

MICTERM-1.r: Term ↔ definition matches: Agar = jelly growth medium; Antibiotic = substance that targets/kills bacteria; Gel sanitiser = high-alcohol hand rub; Microbe = tiny organism (fungi, bacteria, viruses); Control = the sample with no variable applied (checks for contamination); Streak plate = method to get isolated colonies; Sensitivity = affected/killed by something; Resistance = able to withstand it; Blood agar = enriched growth medium; Colony = growth from one microbe; Normal flora = resident body microbes; Leucocyte = white blood cell; UTI = urinary tract infection.
MICTERM-1.q: Define these microbiology terms: agar, antibiotic, gel sanitiser, microbe, control, streak plate, sensitivity, resistance, blood agar, colony, normal flora, leucocyte, UTI.

MICTERM-2.r: Term ↔ definition matches: Swab = sterile sampling tool; Inoculating loop = sterile tool to transfer culture to agar; Inoculate = introduce microbes into a medium; Bacterial culture = growing microbes on medium for ID; Bacterial lawn = confluent growth covering the agar; Inhibition = stopping growth; Zone = an area; Fatal = able to kill; MSU = mid-stream urine sample; Pili = help bacteria stick / pass DNA; Pathogen = a disease-causing microbe; Sterilisation = remove ALL microbes; Disinfect = remove MOST microbes; Sterile = no microbes present; Autoclave = steam steriliser.
MICTERM-2.q: Define these microbiology terms: swab, inoculating loop, inoculate, bacterial culture, bacterial lawn, inhibition, zone, fatal, MSU, pili, pathogen, sterilisation, disinfect, sterile, autoclave.

MICREV-1.r: An autoclave sterilises equipment using pressurised steam (~121°C, 15 psi). The high temperature kills ALL microbes, including heat-resistant endospores.

MICREV-2.r: A disinfectant is a chemical that destroys or removes MOST microbes from non-living surfaces/objects (it may not kill all spores).

MICREV-3.r: An antiseptic is used on LIVING tissue/skin; a disinfectant is used on NON-LIVING surfaces. Antiseptics are gentler because they must not damage tissue.

MICREV-4.r: Not very effective. At ~55°C the water is well below sterilising temperature and contact time is only a few seconds — this just rinses off visible debris. It does not kill most pathogens or spores. Proper cleaning needs detergent + scrubbing, and true killing needs much higher temperatures (e.g. an autoclave/dishwasher sanitise cycle) or a disinfectant.
